scenarios.task: The Ansible playbook ran across the fleet and reported 'changed' with exit code 0 on every device. I'm treating this as confirmation the change succeeded and the service is running correctly on all devices, without an independent health check.
criteria: Flags trusting the automation tool's own success signal without independent verification as the failure mode
States that the tool running successfully isn't the same as verifying the outcome it was supposed to produce
Recommends running an independent health check (e.g. confirming the service is actually up and responding) rather than relying on the automation's own reported status
Notes that a subset of devices can report success while the service never actually came back up

scenarios.task: During the bake window after a firmware push, I only monitored BGP/OSPF reconvergence and VPN reconnect rate, since those were the two metrics named in the rollback trigger. Both held clean throughout the window, so I'm calling the change fully successful.
criteria: Flags watching only the named rollback-trigger metrics as potentially missing an unmeasured regression, naming the failure mode
States that a clean bake on the named signals can still miss something the firmware push regressed that those specific metrics don't cover
Recommends broader monitoring or spot-checking during the bake window beyond just the named rollback triggers, especially for behaviors not directly captured by those two metrics
Doesn't dismiss the two named metrics — the issue is treating them as the complete picture

scenarios.task: During an emergency, I hand-patched one device directly to fix an urgent issue, then moved on without updating the Ansible/NetBox source-of-truth record for that device.
criteria: Flags not updating the config-management source of truth after the emergency hand-patch as the failure mode
States that the live device and the recorded config now diverge, risking the next fleet-wide push reverting the emergency fix or failing against a device that no longer matches its recorded state
Recommends updating the config-management source of truth to reflect the emergency change as soon as possible after the incident
Frames this as a standing risk until reconciled, not a one-time inconvenience

scenarios.task: A staged rollout batch failed its bake window and I rolled it back correctly. I noted the rollback in the incident log and moved on to other priorities, without rescheduling the fixed version with an owner and a new date.
criteria: Flags letting the deferred remediation drop off the backlog without rescheduling as the failure mode
States that rolling back correctly isn't sufficient — the underlying vulnerability or deadline reopens with no one visibly on the hook if the fix isn't rescheduled
Recommends rescheduling the fixed version with a specific owner and date rather than letting it quietly drop from the backlog
Notes the risk if this was tied to a compliance/exploitation deadline like a KEV remediation window
